does: This paper trains transformer language models with most weights pruned to zero, creating weight-sparse networks where each neuron has only a few connections. The authors then apply task-specific pruning to isolate circuits responsible for hand-crafted tasks, finding that these sparse circuits often align with interpretable concepts and straightforward connections. They systematically study the scaling properties of sparse models, quantifying the trade-off between model capability and circuit interpretability, and demonstrate that larger sparse models improve this frontier. The work also explores adapting the sparsity method to explain circuits in existing dense models.
connects: This work builds on longstanding efforts in mechanistic interpretability to reverse-engineer how language models implement their computations, extending prior circuit discovery methods that typically analyze dense networks post-hoc. It combines weight pruning techniques—established in model compression and neural network analysis—with circuit identification to make interpretability more systematic and scalable. The paper directly engages with prior work showing that sparse models can maintain performance and that human-interpretable structure exists in networks, while addressing the challenge that dense model interpretability at scale remains difficult. It responds to the core mechanistic interpretability goal of finding human-understandable computational structures by baking interpretability into training rather than only analyzing post-hoc.
enables: This work points toward interpretable-by-design language models where sparsity constraints are incorporated during training to guarantee understandable circuits. It opens questions about scaling interpretable sparse models beyond tens of millions of parameters and understanding what capability losses are inherent to enforcing interpretability versus contingent on current methods. The preliminary results on explaining dense models suggest a pathway for adapting sparse circuit discovery to probe existing deployed models. Future work could investigate whether task-specific circuits in sparse models reveal fundamental principles about language understanding, whether interpretability-aware training can guide architecture design, and whether sparse circuit understanding enables safer or more controllable language model deployment.
